Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'd try to set aside time on weekends and maybe a couple evenings during the week, but it rarely stuck. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which helped me feel like I was absorbing something even when I wasn't sitting at my desk. With about 14 hours a week dedicated to studying, I thought I was doing okay, but looking back, I spread myself too thin across too many online courses.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due. I'd have good intentions to study regularly, but then extracurriculars would come up or I'd have deadlines for other courses. When things got busy, this course often got pushed aside because I felt like I could catch up later. The weeks before exams looked completely different from normal weeks—suddenly I was cramming everything I'd been putting off.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due or when I got a notification about new content. I wasn't one of those students who checked the LMS daily just to see what was happening. Sometimes I'd log in because I remembered I needed to check a grade or see if there was feedback on something. It was pretty reactive rather than proactive, if I'm being honest with myself.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I tried to make it a habit early in the semester, but it never quite stuck. The gamified tasks actually helped a bit—earning points or badges gave me a little dopamine hit that made me want to come back. But even then, I'd go days without logging in and then binge a bunch of content at once. It never became a natural part of my daily routine the way checking social media is.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I'd watch them at 1.5x speed and really absorb the information that way—I clocked over 100 minutes of video watching because that format just works for my brain. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified concepts I was struggling with. I posted a decent amount and interacted with peers because those conversations made the material feel more real.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just sat there unopened because reading dense text isn't how I learn best. I also barely touched some of the practice quizzes until right before exams, which was probably a mistake. If something wasn't immediately relevant to an assignment or didn't have audio, it often got pushed to the bottom of my priority list and eventually forgotten.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly connected to what I'd be graded on or if it was genuinely interesting. The gamified elements helped too—getting points or seeing my progress on a leaderboard made even boring tasks feel more worthwhile. If I could see how something would help me on an exam or assignment, I was much more likely to engage with it. Activities that felt like busywork with no clear purpose? Those got ignored.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions rather than expectations, so I treated them accordingly. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them, so I'd prioritize those even if I wasn't excited about them. Optional stuff, even if it was probably helpful, always got bumped when I was busy. It's like the difference between a 'must-do' and a 'nice-to-do'—the nice-to-dos rarely survived my packed schedule.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone during commutes or while doing chores. I also had a study group that met in person where we'd discuss concepts without any of us being logged into anything. I used YouTube videos and podcasts related to the course material too, especially when I needed concepts explained in a different way. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my laptop.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd download a lecture, close my laptop, and listen to it three times over the next few days while walking around campus. Or when I'd take handwritten notes from the videos and review those notes repeatedly without ever logging back in. My peer interactions number is decent, but it doesn't capture the hours I spent in study groups face-to-face.
Q11: Yes, all the time. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never click on it again in the system. The LMS probably thinks I only watched something once, but I might have listened to that same lecture five times from my downloads folder. It was just more convenient to have things saved locally so I could study without needing internet or being tied to my computer.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I had exams in multiple courses at once, plus my extracurriculars were demanding a lot of time. I felt this moderate anxiety building up, and instead of pushing through, I kind of froze on this course specifically. It wasn't that I didn't care—I just had to triage, and this course kept getting deprioritized. The material also got more complex around that time, which didn't help.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from other responsibilities, I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but end up just scrolling without really absorbing anything. Confusion was a big factor too—when I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes just close the tab instead of seeking help. My competing responsibilities from extracurriculars and other courses meant this course often got whatever energy I had left, which wasn't much.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but I was really just zoned out, thinking about other things. Or I'd open discussion posts and skim them without really processing what people were saying. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about an assignment for another class or just mentally checked out. The LMS would log that I was 'active,' but I wasn't really there.
Q15: I postponed the assignments way more than I should have, even though my scores on them were actually really good when I did them. I think it was a mix of anxiety about starting and feeling like I needed a big chunk of time to do them properly. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems. The exam studying got postponed the most, which is probably why my exam score was so low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the LMS, downloading everything, watching videos at double speed, posting in discussions. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift away from the course for days. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that consistently got me to engage. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they created mini-deadlines.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep going—my assignment scores were really high, so that positive reinforcement felt good. But feedback on smaller activities was rare or delayed, which made it hard to know if I was on the right track. If I'd gotten more immediate feedback, especially on practice materials, I might have adjusted my exam prep strategy sooner. The lack of feedback made me overconfident about my understanding.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could fit things around my schedule, bad because without strict structure, I'd let things slide. Having ten online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and this course's structure didn't force me to stay on top of things the way some others did. The gamified elements added some structure, but it wasn't enough to keep me consistently engaged.
Q19: The video-heavy format made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner—I really appreciated that. The discussion forums were also well-designed and made me want to participate. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear guidance on what was most important. I sometimes felt overwhelmed by all the resources available and didn't know where to focus my limited time and energy.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to actually produce work. Like, they might be watching videos and reading materials trying to understand, but when it comes to assignments, they're stuck or anxious about submitting something imperfect. Or maybe they're exploring out of curiosity but not connecting that exploration to the actual requirements. It could also be procrastination disguised as productivity.
Q21: That actually resonates with me a bit, especially regarding assignments. My assignment scores were really high even though my LMS activity was pretty low overall. I think it's because I did a lot of learning offline—listening to downloaded lectures, studying with friends, using external resources. The LMS only sees one slice of what students are doing. Someone could be deeply engaged with the material in ways that just don't show up in the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how long you spend on things, and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually thinking or just going through the motions. They miss offline studying, group work, and the mental processing that happens when you're not logged in. They also can't capture why you're doing something, whether it's genuine interest or just checking a box.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in mental effort to understand it, not just completing tasks. It's when the material occupies your thoughts even outside of study time, when you're curious about going deeper. For me, engagement looks like actively listening to lectures, discussing ideas with peers, and connecting concepts to real life. It's not about how many times you log in—it's about whether your brain is actually switched on.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I genuinely enjoyed the discussions with my peers or how the gamified tasks actually made me smile sometimes. It wouldn't capture my study group sessions or the lectures I listened to while walking to class. It also wouldn't show my internal struggle—wanting to do well but feeling pulled in too many directions. My happiness with the learning process and my frustration with my own time management are invisible in the data.
